data.title: Exelon: Strong Buy Amid Data Center Expansion And Regulatory Tailwinds
data.description:
Data Center Supercycle

We initiate Strong Buy $68 PT - Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC) is a premier North American utility holding company with a diversified portfolio of Generation, Transmission and Distribution assets operating throughout a handful of large metropolitan markets. Beneath the shell of a traditional utility is a fundamentally inflecting business - Exelon sits at the intersection of a once-in-a-lifetime data center buildout & intensifying electrification of industry. Consensus would paint Exelon as a painfully slow, rate-case-dependent asset, but more than half of next year’s funding needs are already underwritten, and the comp is primed to deploy $38bn in capital through 2028 to drive the ~7.4% forward rate-base uplift.

Our $9.16bn FY25E base EBITDA (5.7% growth to $9.68bn FY26E) is deliberately substantially below Street - we exclude proximity Maryland noise and assume more disciplined regulatory cadence - but even at this base level, EXC’s mix of newly won RtD rates, density-driven load interconnection and O&M control drive fundamental earnings momentum with each 1% of rate-base growth accruing to EBITDA upside. Our 12.0x FY26E EV/EBITDA target multiple remains modestly below peer median given regulatory discretion but with asymmetric duration sensitivity as Treasury fade and utilities re-rate higher, while financing risk fades with management proactively underwriting ~60% required equity and padding interest expense future macro deterioration. The downside includes adverse regulation and re-accelerating rates, but these are largely priced-in and, in our view, already over-discounted. The asymmetric bear and bull scenarios are startling, with near-term execution de-risked and secular drivers just starting to be priced in. EXC offers one of the most compelling risk/reward set-ups for large cap regulated utilities today.

Data center load explosion

We view Exelon as the epicenter of a structural load transition, driven by accelerating mass data center proliferation across its footprint, and managing a record 17GW pipeline of large load interconnections, and an additional 16GW of high density, front-of-the-meter opportunities under advanced study — each load represents unprecedented capital deployment, regulatory cooperation, and customer engagement across the PJM territory. Our analysis shows these numbers signal not only aggregate utility load step-change, but a new base for multi-year rate-base growth, with 10% of the committed pipeline in-service by 2028 and nearly 75% in-service by 2034, implying a long tail of compounding earnings upside.

Critically, management is clear on none of this growth being tied to FERC-uncertain colocated load — with all projects aligned to incumbent transmission & distribution planning, avoiding ecosystem regulatory uncertainty altogether. The capital intensity is similarly compelling: of the $38bn four-year investment plan, $5bn is allocated to new business (itself a $900mn lift from the prior cycle), with an additional minimum of $1bn of incremental capex for large load explicitly signaled beyond 2028. The market doesn't seem to be fully pricing in large load amid existing uncertainty despite high conversion probability (clustering/ pre-construction engagement with hyperscale customer). We see Exelon's ability to deliver on these projects with proactive customer utilization, accelerated customer interconnect study, and integrated planning as a structural advantage that can deliver long duration above peer earnings growth constrained by permitting and regulatory support. We believe this can drive multi-year re-rating and outright visibility not yet appropriately valued.

Balance Sheet Readiness

We believe that Exelon's capital strategy rapidly becomes a central competitive advantage underappreciated by valuation, with the balance of forward equity requirements for the next fiscal year almost 60% locked or committed, on a pace meaningfully ahead of large-cap utility peers and exposed to capital market volatility. This disciplined execution is reflected in the transparent matching of the $38bn capex plan with near-term financing and hedging, highlighted by unusual visibility on both rate-base growth and the costs of fulfillment.

We find that the succession of equity raises and refinancing tranches secured ahead of tightening liquidity landscapes represents the elimination of a core element of balance sheet risk the market has been quick to price for the space. With balance sheet fragility driving uniform scrutiny of the sector as interest rates rise, Exelon's readiness is unmatched and suggests both tactical and strategic moats. Our conviction is informed by a broader view that de-risked financing execution, not usually appreciated until stress is tested, is here underwritten by management's ahead-of-the-tide positioning and transparent disclosure, mitigating downside and a lever to outperformance as macro headwinds ease and the utility re-rating thesis comes into view.

Exelon: Regulated CapEx Platform underpinning visibility, growing vs. prior year but less than Street

We estimate FY25E Exelon EBITDA at $9.16bn (vs. $9.54bn the street; +12.0% y/y) and FY26E at $9.68bn (vs. $9.83bn the street; +5.7% y/y), a -4.0% and -1.5% haircut to street consensus, respectively. Our haircut to consensus is a deliberate one: we exclude Maryland reconciliations ($120mn of one-time major settlements baked into several sell-side models) and we assume two significant rate cases close later than the street, delaying new revenue recognition.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $24.06B 6 $24.80B 8
EBITDA $9.54B 6 $9.83B 8
Net Income $2.71B 6 $2.82B 8
EPS $2.70 12 $2.82 12
SG&A Expenses $50.24M 6 $51.77M 8

Instead, we see the $38bn 2025-28 Exelon capex guide and resulting 7.4% annual x-rate base growth as the primary earnings driver, with each 1% of incremental rate-base expansion flowing straight through to EBITDA given timely regulated cost recovery. Near-term, we see an upside locked in via new awarded D&T rates alone (+$450mn FY25E EBITDA on an annualized basis), while high-density-load customer connects, and disciplined O&M oversight, should deliver additional margin gains and reigning in broad inflation, with FY O&M +0.5% y/y (+$100mn of sustainable savings embedded). We also see minimal financing risk baked in, with >50% of FY25 debt and 60% of equity guidance already secured, capping interest expense sensitivity. Overall, we see our EBITDA estimates offering a deliberate haircut to consensus yet supported by highly visible, regulated earnings flows—not the least on timing and normalization items, whereby we think the street is prematurely calling shots.

Valuation

Our $68 PT on Exelon is set to a 12.0x FY26E EV/EBITDA multiple to our $9.68bn EBITDA forecast (+29% upside, disciplined premium multiple selection). The forward multiple, set slightly below the peer median multiple (12.2x) and 83rd pctile of its own history (6.9x), is an expression of conviction that the market grossly underestimates the sustainability of EXC's positive catalysts and changing R/R profile. We believe that the company's unique exposure to the data center supercycle, coupled with accelerating transmission spend and a $38bn capex program through 2028, support a 5.7% EBITDA CAGR through FY26E, with each incremental rate-base expansion flowing directly to the bottom line.

Our multiple stays below the peer set to reflect residual regulatory and macro uncertainty, however also captures both the reduced funding risk (with over half of 2025 equity already locked in) and the sizable multiyear capital backlog of the company. We see the current discount to peers as unjustified with an EPS profile that has shifted to above-average visible growth; at these levels, this is a trade set-up for the medium term with an eye on inflection of sector-wide dynamics.

Risks to our Strong Buy Thesis

Despite our presentation of an asymmetrically attractive R/R Exelon narrative centered around inflecting load demand and outsized regulatory and capex tailwinds, we believe the latter could be undermined by a combination of exogenous and company-specific factors. Most importantly, EXC's growth is levered to the execution and timely approval of outsized multi-jurisdictional ratemaking expansion plans—any meaningful adverse regulatory outcome in key states like Illinois or Maryland (recent history includes rejected grid plans and as low as 8.91% ROE at ComEd, well below utility requests) can inflect authorized returns, capex recovery and ultimately incite downscaling of the investment ambition, rein in the forecast upside.

Similarly, while EXC's capital structure rebalancing is in a de-risking phase with over 50% of 2025's equity needs secured, the sector remains highly sensitive to interest rate volatility and cost inflation; to the extent macro conditions worsen and/or regulatory fatigue on further rate relief takes hold, cost pass-through mechanisms or recovery lags can reduce near-term EPS momentum and further multiple pressure. We also highlight the risk that secular datacenter demand does not materialize as projected, or that behind-the-meter solutions (storage/rooftop solar) offset much of the forecasted 1–2% retail load growth—metrics like GW interconnection backlogs, transmission execution cadence, and actual vs targeted load growth should be tracked as leading indicators. In sum, our constructive outlook remains, but monitoring regulation outcomes, actual cost inflation, capital plan execution, and the realization of load ramp are critical to validate or challenge our Strong Buy Recommendation.

Conclusion

We see Exelon as the cutting edge of a unique multi-year re-rating driven by durable data center demand, disciplined capex deployment, and visible regulation-backing EBITDA runway...all not fully baked in at its valuation targets, in our view. Our conviction is anchored by Exelon's ability to directly convert rate-base expansion into earnings growth while proactively de-risking funding (asymmetric setup vs an already discounted regulatory backdrop). For investors desiring quality exposure to secular electrification and the utilities sector's 'next big thing,' we see EXC as a compelling medium-term Strong Buy, with execution on capital plans and tangible demand ramp the key catalysts to watch.

data.title: Synopsys: Wait And Watch
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) is a market-leading provider of semiconductor design software (EDA) and IP, poised to benefit from powerful secular tailwinds in chip developments by AI, electrification and designing. The company’s operational and business prospects look impressive, with a 2024 revenue growth rate of 15% ($6.127 billion) with 25% Earnings Per Share (EPS) growth. The FCFE DCF valuation (8.55% discount rate, 17% growth rate and 4% terminal growth rate) yields an equity value of 53 billion dollars ($341 per share), well below the current market capitalisation of 75 billion dollars ($486 per share). A wait-and-watch stance is recommended at present. However, the European Commission's approval of the acquisition of Ansys is a transformational factor, as it expands Synopsys’ market and boosts the operating margins. After being officially acquired by Synopsys (mostly within the first half of 2025), a significant upside can be anticipated with an initial target of $550 per share, supported by enhanced growth and profitability. Synopsys is a high-quality firm in a booming sector, but patience for a better intrinsic valuation hedges the risk for the bullish players and long-term investors. A "wait and watch" or "hold" is recommended.

Company Overview

Synopsys Inc. (SNPS) provides Electronic Design Automation [EDA] software products, used for enabling efficient integrated circuits. The company operates in the sectors of Design Automation and Design IP. EDA includes digital and custom IC (Integrated Circuit) design software, verification hardware and software products, manufacturing-related designing products, FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array) design software, AI-driven EDA solutions and Professional services. Design IP includes interface, foundation, security, embedded processor IP, IP subsystems and IP implementation services. Currently employing 20,000 individuals, SNPS controls around one-third of the EDA market.

Unique Selling Propositions

Duopoly Power & Customer Lock-In

Synopsys’s competitive moat is exceptionally strong, underpinned by its duopoly market position, deep integration in customer workflows and relentless innovation. Synopsys and Cadence (CDNS) dominate the EDA software market, each holding roughly one-third of the market, leaving only around 13% to the next competitor. Crucially, EDA tools are so mission-critical that SNPS almost “never loses a client”, resulting in a near 100% retention with 80-85% recurring revenue. Once a chipmaker adopts Synopsys’s platform, switching costs and the risk of disruption keep them locked in. The result is a highly resilient business with long-term customer relationships and pricing power.

Technological Product Portfolio

Technology leadership further fuels SNP’s edge. The company offers the industry’s broadest portfolio of chip designs and verification solutions that includes tools for logic synthesis, simulation, physical design and a large library of silicon IP blocks. Its scale in semiconductor design IP is a differentiator, as it generates more than 25% of revenue (Figure 1.1).

Figure 1.1

AI-Driven Product Innovation

SNPS has been a pioneer in applying AI and ML to its chip design. This is a critical development as circuits grow in complexity. Tools such as DSO.ai for autonomous design space optimisation and VSO.ai for verification and gaining traction. In Q1 2025, SNPS introduced new generative AI capabilities embedded in its EDA stack, with strong demand at advanced semiconductor nodes. SNPS shows promising ability to stay technologically ahead.

'Silicon to Systems' Strategy with Ansys Acquisition

A major differentiator for SNPS is its evolving “Silicon to Systems” strategy (refer to figure 1.2), soon to be strengthened by Ansys acquisition. Having partnered successfully with for more than 7 years, Synopsys and Ansys aim to offer an integrated platform combining electronic design automation. This uniquely addresses the complexity of intelligent systems, where chip performance must be co-optimised with thermal electromagnetic, and mechanical constrains.

Figure 1.2

Market Expansion

The merger is expected to significantly expand Synopsys’s total addressable market to around $28 billion at 11% CAGR. This generates both cross-selling opportunities and operational efficiencies. Management projects a targeted improvement of 250 basis points on non-GAAP operating margin in the medium term.

Intrinsic Valuation (FCFE DCF)

Despite Synopsys’s enviable business quality, the current valuation (June 2025) suggests the stock’s current price embeds very optimistic assumptions. A detailed DCF [FCFE] analysis (Figure 1.3) based on the key inputs of cost of equity of 8.44% (Figure 1.4), 5-year growth rate of 17%, terminal growth rate of 4% (considering operational excellence and technology USPs), FCFE of $1.3 billion (2024) gives a share price value of $350.61. This intrinsic value is significantly lower (30% overvaluation) than Synopsys’s actual market value ($77.69 billion) and share price ($500.76 as of 12th June 2025). The gap implies overvaluation on a fundamental cash flow basis.

Free cash flow generation is robust as SNPS consistently converts a high portion of earnings to cash and the balance sheet is healthy, and the balance sheet looks healthy (operating cash flow of $1.8 billion and free cash flow of $1.6 billion, Q4 2024).

However, the current valuation suggests a “wait and watch” stance. The conservation 4% terminal growth and mid-teens growth assumptions presume SNPS’ double-digit sustainability well beyond 2025. Any growth deceleration could lead to multiple contractions. While Synopsys’s financial performance is strong and the balance sheet can support strategic investments, the stock’s upside seems limited in the near term by an overly rich valuation. Waiting for a fundamental catalyst (for instance, growth from new markets or realised success of Ansys acquisition) or a pullback in share price to better align with intrinsic value before an aggressive buying. Currently, the overall rating favours “hold” for the investors and interested stakeholders.

Risks

Acquisition Risk

The $35 billion proposed acquisition of Ansys introduces significant execution uncertainty. The deal, structured as a mix of cash and stock, is expected to dilute existing equity holders and increase debt. Achieving targeted cost synergies and cross-selling opportunities (such as the projected 250 basis points uplift in operational margins) is crucial for successful integration. Complexity of regulatory approvals is another vital risk as FTC has mandated divestiture of certain assets to preserve competition, and any delays in closing beyond the H1 2025 target could introduce market volatility.

Valuation and Market Risk

With Synopsys trading at around 40x forward earnings, the stock is priced for perfection. Any signs of growth deceleration due to macro headwinds or Research and Development spending from clients could lead to a sharp correction. In June 2025, Synopsys suspended financial guidance due to uncertainties surrounding U.S. export controls, underlining its sensitivity to geopolitical risk.

Geopolitical and Regulatory Risk

Approximately 16% of Synopsys’s revenue comes from China. As U.S. Vs China tensions escalate, tighter export restrictions on EDA software could limit SNPS’ addressable market. Export bans on advanced chip design tools prompted the company to pause guidance in May 2025. This shows the importance of global trade policies.

Earnings Analysis

Recent earnings have been solid overall. In Q1 FY2025, the company reported a slightly down (3.7% Year-On-Year) revenue of $1.511 billion. Notably, non-GAAP EPS for Q1 was $3.03, reaffirming its full-year outlook for double-digit growth after Q1. Second Quarter FY2025 validated a return to growth by reported revenue of $1.604 billion (10% Year-On-Year) in the prior-year quarter. In Q2, CEO Ghazi highlighted “We delivered a strong quarter, demonstrating the mission-critical nature of our products and the resiliency of our business.”

It is vital to note that subsequent to Q2 results, new export control developments prompted SNPS to suspend forward guidance in June. The U.S. government’s updated rules on EDA tool exports to China have introduced uncertainty in the second half outlook.

Overall, Synopsys’s H1 for this fiscal year (2025) performance reflects healthy growth, supporting the view of its excellent business operations, yet it also reveals how external factors (trade policy) can introduce volatility.

Competitor Analysis: Cadence

Undoubtedly, SNPS’ primary competitor in the EDA market in Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), with whom it shares a comfortable duopoly. Also considered as the “Coke Vs Pepsi” of the EDA market, each firm offers a broad suite of EDA tools, and many customers use both to some extent. In terms of market share, both hold one-third of the market.

Synopsys is larger, with FY2024 revenue $6.1 billion Cadence’s $4.64 billion. SNPS has a more diversified business due to its significant IP licensing segment, which Cadence lacks at. This gives SNPS as edge in addressing the trend toward reusable IP and chip design. Cadence, on the other hand, has traditionally enjoyed higher operating margins (around 40% non-GAAP in 2024 Vs SNPS’ 30%). Both companies can be viewed as high-growth, high-quality names in the market.

In terms of product competition, both companies have their unique strengths. SNPS is leading in the digital implementation, IP blocks and Verification solutions, while Cadence is strong in analogue/mixed-signal design. Both are embracing AI tools (Cadence has its Cerebrus AI design tool).

Altogether, Synopsys’s position in extremely strong, especially given the near-term closed deal on the acquisition of Ansys. This will not only potentially outscale Cadence but also leap ahead in offering multi-physics system design capabilities (something Cadence would find difficult to replicate).

Who is This Right Fit For?

By now, you might have guessed it right, SNPS is best suited for long-term growth investors as it offers a rare combination of stable, recurring software revenue and secular growth drivers (also making it attractive for institutional investors). However, given the current “wait and watch” stance according to the intrinsic valuation, the stock may not appeal to value-oriented investors at the moment. The ideal investor for Synopsys at this juncture is someone who believes in the long-term demand for EDA and IP. For a more conservative investor, SNPS’ overvaluation and trade policies add risk in the short term.

Conclusion

Synopsys stands out as a premier player in the semiconductor industry, underpinned by market leadership, long-term clients, and cutting-edge technology (AI-driven chip design trends). The Ansys acquisition could enhance its long-term growth trajectory and competitive edge (given that the associated risks are managed at a satisfactory level). However, current valuations appear stretched, already pricing in much of this optimism. The fundamentals are strong for SNPS, but the valuation and the current market conditions support patience.

A wait and watch stance is recommended for new investors. However, a more compelling buy case could emerge following either a successful Ansys integration or a market correction bringing the stock closer to fair value. In essence, SNPS remains a strong long-term holding, but disciplined entry timing is crucial to maximise future returns.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Overlooked Growth And A Smart Buy For Patient Investors Willing To Wait
data.description:
Why Exxon Has My Attention

Exxon Mobil (XOM) isn’t anyone’s idea of a sexy growth story right now. Headlines are messy. Last quarter, Q1 profit was a letdown. Analyst revisions are negative across the board. And yet, I keep circling back to it, because I don’t think the market is seeing the full picture—or at least, it’s not connecting the dots beyond the near-term drama.

Here’s where my head is at: There’s genuine risk here (regulatory, legal, political—you name it) but also a pretty rare setup for long-term upside. Exxon has just pulled off the biggest US energy deal in ages (Pioneer), Guyana posted a 64% profit jump and they’re quietly building out a lower-carbon business that—if it actually delivers—could be a game-changer. The valuation? Not screamingly cheap but more than reasonable for the free cash flow and assets you’re getting. I’m giving this a Buy (not pounding the table but definitely interested), with a 12–24 month time horizon.

What’s Happening Under the Hood

Exxon is still the heavyweight champ among the oil majors. They’ve got global scale and more importantly, a mix of old-school “moat” assets and fresh growth engines (the Permian via Pioneer and offshore Guyana). That Pioneer deal from last year? They paid up, but it locks in decades of low-cost US shale inventory and, in theory, sets them up to ride any US energy resurgence harder than anyone else.

Financially, the picture’s mixed. Revenue for Q1 2025 came in at $83.1B, more or less flat. EPS was $1.76, down from $2.06 last year. Margins are still respectable for a company this size (about 12% EBIT, close to 10% net) but there’s pressure from higher depreciation and costs tied to all these new projects and the big acquisition. The dividend is still untouchable ($3.96 annualized, yield near 3.5%) and they’ve kept up their buybacks—but the payout ratio is getting high and if energy prices don’t bounce, that becomes a headache.

They’ve got $17B in cash, so there’s no liquidity issue, but debt jumped after the Pioneer deal (total debt now ~$37.6B). ROE is solid at 14% and they’re still making money even in a tougher environment. Free cash flow is a little soft right now—again, mostly capex and integration costs.

Oil Majors: Out of Favor but Cycles Change

Big picture, this whole sector has been out of favor. Oil prices aren’t in crisis, but they’re nowhere near “boom” territory and refining margins are weak—especially outside North America. Exxon’s US-heavy footprint is actually a plus right now and their chemical business is basically at the bottom of the cycle (so, not much further to fall, in my opinion).

Regulatory and legal risks are front and center. The Supreme Court is set to hear climate cases involving Exxon and others; multiple states are coming after them for everything from water pollution to heat wave deaths (legal/ESG headwinds). Politicians are as unpredictable as ever. Meanwhile, Exxon is trying to thread the needle—still growing oil output but leaning harder into “lower-carbon” tech and partnerships (think ammonia, hydrogen, CCS, etc.), hoping to hedge their bets as the world changes.

Is the Market Getting This Wrong?

At $113, you’re looking at a PE of 14.9 (trailing) and a forward multiple around 17, based on consensus estimates. That’s neither a screaming bargain nor expensive—about average for Big Oil these days. EV/EBITDA is just under 8x. Price/book at 1.85x is pretty reasonable given the asset quality. And yet, the analyst sentiment is sour (22 estimate cuts this year, just 2 increases) and it feels like everyone is either underweight or just not paying attention.

The kicker for me: The Street expects revenue to grow from $326B this year to $356B by 2027 and for EPS to jump from $6.55 to $9.50 in that window. So, even with all the legal noise, Wall Street’s still modeling nearly 45% EPS growth over three years. Are those numbers too rosy? Maybe, but even if you haircut them, Exxon’s free cash flow yield isn’t bad and the downside looks contained unless oil completely rolls over.

Peer check: Here’s what jumps out when you line up Exxon, Chevron and Shell side by side. Exxon’s trading at about 15 times earnings (17 forward), which is basically neck-and-neck with Chevron—Chevron’s actually a tad pricier on forward PE. Shell comes in cheaper (about 11.6x forward), but that discount probably reflects its lower profitability and the fact that its three-year EPS trend is actually negative.

ROE is where Exxon really stands out—14% compared to 10% for Chevron and just under 8% for Shell. Same story on margins: Exxon’s EBIT margin is a little better than both of them. Shell’s dividend yield is the highest (almost 4%), Chevron’s isn’t far behind, but Exxon’s payout ratio is way more manageable than Chevron’s, which is pushing 87%.

All three are feeling the squeeze on free cash flow this year—nobody’s immune—but when I look at what could change the narrative, Exxon’s got the clearest path: the Guyana ramp and the Pioneer deal are actually needle-movers, not just marketing bullet points. So while none of these stocks are “cheap” in the classic sense, Exxon’s got the better balance of quality assets and future upside, at least in my view.

What the Headlines Are Missing

Honestly, the past few months have been a mess. Q1 profit was a letdown. There are lawsuits flying in every direction and the press coverage reflects that. On the flip side, the actual operational news has been quietly solid: Guyana posted a 64% profit jump; Exxon’s racking up new deals from Azerbaijan to India; and they’re still getting invited to every big industry conference. The market, though, has been quick to punish every negative surprise and mostly shrugs at the good news.

Bottom line: Investors seem to be focused on legal/ESG headwinds and policy whiplash. The day-to-day stock movement is more about macro and headline anxiety than anything Exxon is (or isn’t) executing on.

Risks

The big ones are:

  • Legal/ESG headwinds: The lawsuits are real. Exxon claims the risk is “remote,” but no one truly knows how these cases will shake out. Even a small legal loss could set an expensive precedent.

  • Commodity price weakness: If oil spends the next two years in the $60s, cash flows get tight. The dividend is safe for now but not bulletproof forever.

  • Integration slip-ups: Pioneer is a big bite and history says these deals often disappoint if integration stumbles or synergies are slow.

  • Policy whiplash: Energy policy, both in the US and abroad, is just unpredictable. Today’s project can be canceled with the next election.

  • ESG/political backlash: As environmental lawsuits multiply, it’s just harder to run an oil company without getting tangled up in negative press and possible restrictions.

Exxon’s approach is to fight back hard, bet on its scale and keep cash flowing. That might work but there’s no guarantee.

What Could Actually Move the Stock

Scenarios:

Bear Case ($95): Oil stays weak, legal/ESG headwinds get worse and synergies from Pioneer disappoint. EPS stalls out; dividend growth slows or stops.

Base Case ($130): Oil stabilizes in the $75–85 range, Pioneer and Guyana deliver as planned and most lawsuits get delayed or quietly settled. EPS climbs to the $9+ range by 2027. The market finally warms up to the story.

Bull Case ($150+): Something actually goes right for a change: oil rallies, Guyana posted a 64% profit jump and surprises to the upside and Exxon's low-carbon bets start to bear fruit. Street upgrades pile in and the stock re-rates to the top of its cycle.

How I’d Play It

So, am I buying? Yeah, I’m interested—especially in weakness. If you can get shares in the $105–$110 range, I think you’re getting paid to wait for better days, with a solid dividend and plenty of upsides if the cycle turns. I’m not pretending this is a home run—there are real, non-trivial risks. But the market’s already pricing in a lot of pain and not much potential upside.

If I had to sum it up: Exxon is a classic “contrarian but not crazy” buy. There are easier stories out there but few with this much optionality and so much investor skepticism already baked in. If you want a set-it-and-forget-it, sleep-well-at-night name—this isn’t it. But for patient investors, the risk/reward is finally getting interesting again.

data.title: BoE Freezes Rates At 4.25% While CPI Re-Accelerates
data.description:
The Bank of England’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) kept its main Bank Rate steady at 4.25 percent yesterday, resisting a growing chorus for relief even as inflation flared again and growth sputtered. Minutes published Thursday reveal a 6–3 split - the most dissent since 2020 - with Swati Dhingra, Dave Ramsden, and newcomer Alan Taylor pushing for a 25 basis point cut.

Yet Governor Andrew Bailey’s majority held the line, citing “heightened unpredictability” in both the economic and geopolitical sphere. Traders looking for a quick easing cycle were left disappointed, and spot gold promptly pierced US$3,374 per ounce, underscoring investor unease with fiat promises.

The hawkish camp argued that cooling too soon risks entrenching price pressures that have proven sticky. Twelve-month Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation jumped to 3.4 percent in May from 2.6 percent in March, while core CPI ticked up to 3.5 percent.

Services inflation, a gauge closely watched by the MPC, remained an uncomfortable 4.7 percent. Brent crude’s 26 percent surge since May, coupled with an 11 percent rise in European natural-gas prices, suggests cost-push forces aren’t done yet. Although the Bank projects inflation will “drift toward” the 2 percent target in 2026, that horizon keeps inching outward - hardly reassuring for households whose purchasing power has already been clipped.

The BoE Bank Rate remains at a 2-year low, but pressure is building to reduce rates.

Doves countered that the real economy is losing altitude. After a solid 0.7 percent GDP gain in the first quarter, output shrank 0.3 percent in April, and the Bank now sees only 0.25 percent growth for Q2. Payrolled employment fell by 109,000 in May, vacancy rates slipped below equilibrium, and private sector regular pay growth slowed to 5.1 percent.

Most new wage settlements sit between 3 and 4 percent, a trajectory the minutes say is “consistent with a significant decline in wage growth” later this year. With the European Central Bank already trimming rates and the Federal Reserve on pause, dissenters feared the U.K. may be tightening into a slowdown.

Market participants remain skeptical that Threadneedle Street can keep rates elevated for long. The Bank’s own survey shows traders still pricing roughly 50 basis points of cuts by year-end, and inflation only returning to target on a three-year view.

Meanwhile, the central bank is sitting on a £590 billion gilt mountain - a legacy of the last crisis that quietly expands the money supply whenever proceeds are reinvested. It is little wonder that gold, the classical hedge against monetary experimentation, keeps making new highs even as crypto darlings languish.

“Monetary policy is not on a pre-set path,” the minutes insist, but the widening split among committee members hints at mounting unease over that very lack of direction. For savers and borrowers alike, the message is clear: policy makers are flying through turbulence with limited visibility, and the seat-belt sign may stay lit for some time to come.

data.title: Novo Nordisk And Its Real Value
data.description:
Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF) has been recently in a controversy with the recent removal of CEO Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen on May 16, 2025. The share price decline and recent market challenges attributed to initiating a CEO succession as “the best interest of the company and its shareholders”.

Goldman Sachs has recently cut its forecasts for anti-obesity drugs, especially the injectable versions of GLP 1-based drugs, to hit $95 billion by 2030, a significant decrease from an earlier estimate of $130 billion.

Novo Nordisk’s business model is still relatively robust. Novo has obtained significant market dominance in GLP-1 treatments, specifically its best-selling obesity medicine Wegovy and diabetes treatment Ozempic. On June 11th, Deep Apple Therapeutics, a privately held biotech company, announced a licensing deal with Novo Nordisk. Novo Nordisk’s other acquisitions include Catalent manufacturing facilities. This acquisition showcases a strategy of long-term improved capacity growth.

Aside from Novo Nordisk, Eli Lilly (LLY) and Pfizer (PFE) are the two other biggest pharmaceutical players that specialize in weight-loss drugs.

When considering these current stories about Novo Nordisk, we need to determine which news topics will have a long-term and ongoing effect on the company and its share price.

This article will focus on the long-term fundamentals of the company, which tend to give us a better picture of the company as a viable investment. I will also analyze the value of the company versus the price and help you to determine if Novo Nordisk is currently trading at a bargain price. I provide various situations which help estimate the company's future returns. In closing, I will tell you my personal opinion about whether I'm interested in taking a position in this company and why.

Snapshot Of The Company

A fast way for me to get an overall understanding of the condition of the business is to use the BTMA Stock Analyzer’s company rating score. Novo Nordisk A/S has an impressive company rating score of 87 out of 100. In summary, NVO seems to have above-average fundamentals.

Before jumping to conclusions, we’ll have to look closer into individual categories to see what’s going on.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer)

Fundamentals

Let’s examine the price per share history first. In the chart below, we can see that the price per share was flat in the first few years, then in 2020 the share price began gradually increasing. It has continued this upward path up to 2025. Overall, the share price average has grown by about 436.4% over the past 10 years or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 20.52%. This is a very good return.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Price Per Share History)

Earnings

Looking closer at earnings history, we see that earnings have consistently grown over the past 10 years, with not a single year of earnings declines. This is not an easy feat to achieve.

Novo Nordisk is a global pharmaceutical leader specializing in diabetes and obesity care, with strong demands for its GLP-1 treatment such as Ozempic and Wegovy. As public health awareness has grown in recent years and obesity rates continue to rise worldwide, the company has seen steady growth in its core treatment categories.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – EPS History)

Since earnings and price per share don’t always give the whole picture, it’s good to look at other factors like the gross margins, return on equity, and return on invested capital.

Return On Equity

The return on equity has remained strong from 2020 to 2024. There was a noticeable spike in ROE in 2023 (88.07). For return on equity (ROE), I look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, NVO crushes this guideline.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – ROE History)

Let’s compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of 231 Pharmaceutical companies is 10.49%.

Therefore, NVO’s 5-year average of 76.358% is well above industry standards.

Return On Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has its ups and downs from 2020 to 2024. ROIC fell nearly 9.05% during the period, but the 5-year average ROIC is above expectations at around 58.71%. For return on invested capital [ROIC], I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, NVO is an outstanding performer in terms of ROIC as well.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Return on Invested Capital History)

Gross Margin Percent

The gross margin percent [GMP] has steadily improved over the past four years, rising from 83.20% in 2021 to 84.67% in 2024. The five-year average GMP stands at 83.98%, which shows consistent profitability. I typically look for companies with gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, NVO has proven that it has the ability to maintain acceptable margins over a long period.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Gross Margin Percent History)

Financial Stability

Looking at other fundamentals involving the balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity is less than 1. This is a positive indicator, telling us that the company owns more than it owes.

Novo Nordisk’s current ratio of 0.73 could use improvement. It indicates that the company may not have an adequate ability to use its assets to pay its short-term debt.

Ideally, we’d want to see a Current Ratio of more than 1, otherwise it could be a sign of limited short-term liquidity.

According to the balance sheet, the company appears to be in relatively stable financial health, other than a small improvement needed for the current ratio.

NVO has a history of paying interim and final dividends, usually paid out twice per year.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Misc. Fundamentals)

This analysis wouldn’t be complete without considering the value of the company vs. share price.

Value Vs. Price

The company’s Price-Earnings Ratio of 20.44 indicates that NVO might be selling at a high price when comparing NVO’s PE Ratio to a long-term market average PE Ratio of 15.

The 10-year and 5-year average PE Ratio of NVO has typically been 27 and 32, respectively. This indicates that NVO could be currently trading at a low price when comparing to its average historical PE Ratio range.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Stock Value)

The Estimated Value of the Stock is $94.81, versus the current stock price of $70.07. This indicates that NVO is currently selling at a bargain price.

For more detailed valuation purposes, I will be using an EPS of 3.38. I’ve used various past averages of growth rates and PE Ratios to calculate different scenarios of valuation ranges from low to average values. The valuations compare growth rates of EPS, Book Value, and Total Equity.

In the table below, you can see the different scenarios, and in the chart, you will see vertical valuation lines that correspond to the table valuation ranges. The dots on the lines represent the current stock price. If the dot is towards the bottom of the valuation range, this would indicate that the stock is undervalued. If the dot is near the top of the valuation line, this would show an overvalued stock.

(Source: BTMA Wealth Builders Club)

This analysis shows an average valuation of around $92 per share versus its current price of about $74, this would indicate that Novo Nordisk is undervalued.

However, the valuation based on low forward growth indicates a possibility that the stock could fall lower to a price of $65-$67 in the short term.

Summarizing The Fundamentals

According to the facts, Novo Nordisk is financially healthy in the long term with a debt-to-equity ratio under 1. In the short term, its current ratio of 0.73 is slightly below the ideal benchmark, which means it has slightly limited liquidity to cover its liabilities.

This company has an impressive earnings record, which shows consistent growth over the past 10 years.

Other fundamentals are at excellent levels, including ROE, ROIC, and Gross Margins. The only shortcoming is its current ratio (0.73), which falls below the preferred minimum of 1.

Another consideration is Novo Nordisk’s leadership in diabetes and obesity care along with growing demands due to health trends and expanding public health campaigns globally. As awareness around health arises, Novo Nordisk is well positioned to maintain continued growth in the foreseeable future.

In terms of valuation, my analysis shows that the stock is undervalued.

Novo Nordisk Vs. The S&P 500

Now, let’s see how NVO compares versus the US stock market benchmark S&P 500 over the past 10 years. From the chart below, we can see that NVO stayed below the S&P 500 and only briefly outperformed the market from about 2023 to 2025.

This tells me that NVO might not be a better long-term investment than a low fee S&P 500 index fund. But if you decide to buy NVO, then you should make certain to buy at a big discount to provide a better possible return than the market index fund.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts that follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 14.41%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is at $91.57, which is about a 23.73% increase in a year.

The Expected Annual Compounding Rate of Return is 15.50%.

If you invest today, with analysts’ forecasts, you might expect about 4% (low growth) to 14% (average growth) per year.

Here is an alternative scenario based on NVO’s past earnings growth. During the past 10 and 5-year periods, the average EPS growth rate was about 11.7% and 15.4%, respectively.

But when considering cash flow growth over the past 10 and 5 years, the average growth has been 9.1% and 5.7%, respectively.

Therefore, when considering all of these return possibilities, our average annual return could likely be around 12%-14%.

If considering actual past results of Novo Nordisk, the story is a bit different. Here are the actual 10 and 5-year return results.

______________

10 Year Return Results if Invested in NVO:

Initial Investment Date: 6/20/2015

End Date: 6/20/2025

Cost per Share: $27.49

End Date Price: $73.77

Total Dividends Received: $8.29

Total Return: 198.51%

Compound Annualized Growth Rate: 12%

_______________

5 Year Return Results if Invested in NVO:

Initial Investment Date: 6/20/2020

End Date: 6/20/2025

Cost per Share: $33.77

End Date Price: $73.77

Total Dividends Received: $5.37

Total Return: 134.32%

Compound Annualized Growth Rate: 19%

_________________

From these scenarios, we have produced results from 12% to 19%. I feel that if you’re a long-term patient investor and believer in NVO, and its existing products (diabetes and obesity treatments), you could expect NVO to provide you with around at least 12% annual return over the long haul. But for the short-term swing trader or impatient investor, the near future of NVO, might show some volatility (especially in the US market) from initiatives to lower drug costs. Other concerns are the negative views about Ozempic and Wegovy by US Secretary of Health, Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., and negative news about the potential side effects of the drugs.

Does Novo Nordisk Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? YES (87)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? YES (20.5%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? YES
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (76.3%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (58.7%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (83.9%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? NO
  9. Outperformed S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? NO
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? YES

NVO scored 8/10 or 80%. Therefore, NVO is worth considering as a potential investment!

Is NVO Currently Selling At A Bargain Price?

  1. Detailed Valuation greater than Current Stock Price YES (Value 91.68 > $73.77 Stock Price)
  2. Estimated Value greater than Current Stock Price? YES (Value $94.81 > $73.77 Stock Price)

From my analysis, Novo Nordisk appears to be a suitable investment because it is fundamentally sound with strong fundamental performance of key metrics.

In my opinion, buying this stock at a deep discount is the key. Otherwise, if you pay too much for this stock, it is less likely to outperform an S&P 500 index fund.

Buying at a deep discount would also help to offset risks. One main risk I see is the Inflation Reduction Act’s initiative to lower pharmaceutical costs, which could significantly affect Novo Nordisk’s bottom line. This is a big deal, since the USA market makes up about 58% of the company’s geographic revenue.

To understand how much this could affect Novo Nordisk, let’s look at the company’s NovoLog insulin drug and how it was affected by the Inflation Reduction Act. The price was negotiated down to $119, from its initial list price of $495. That’s a reduction of about 75%.

Price negotiations are underway for Ozempic and Wegovy and there should be some decision made by the end of 2025. Therefore, my current plan is to wait until some decision is made on the price of these drugs which make up about 60% of Novo Nordisk’s product revenue.

Thereafter, Novo Nordisk should release an adjusted guidance based on any price changes. This would help to determine what price should be paid for the stock. The news surrounding the possible drug price reductions might also provide a shockwave that could temporarily lower the stock price to a deeper discount for buying. I will wait patiently to see how this unfolds.

data.title: Trouble Ahead, Says The Fed
data.description:
Relative to how these things can sometimes go, the Federal Open Market Committee’s meeting this week was a bit of a nonevent for markets. The Fed was widely expected to keep interest rates where they have been since last September, with a Fed funds rate upper bound of 4.5 percent, and that is precisely what happened.

The FOMC meeting concluded, and the trading day ended with not much more than a shrug from the stock and bond markets. But below the headline takeaway of no change in rates, there was plenty to suggest that the central bankers are not particularly happy with what they see in the economy as the second half of the year gets underway.

Dot Plot Tea Leaves

The FOMC’s Summary Economic Projections, colloquially known as the “dot plot,” reflected a change from the previous reading back in March, and the change was essentially in one direction: worse. Inflation is projected to be higher, and so is unemployment, while real GDP growth estimates are lower (bear in mind that these are just estimates, subject to changes in actual conditions, not to mention good old-fashioned human error).

The picture isn’t exactly doom and gloom – the numbers don’t spell out a likely recession – but the median outlook for GDP growth in 2025 is 1.4 percent, down from the March estimate of 1.7 percent. Inflation, as measured by the Core Personal Consumption Expenditure index, is expected to register at 3.1 percent by the end of the year, up from the prior forecast of 2.7 percent.

None of this is particularly surprising, given that the March FOMC meeting took place before the full tariff-related mayhem that kicked off on April 2. But it is a good reminder that, even if we avoid the worst possible outcomes in terms of the tariff policy’s effect, economic conditions are likely to get worse before they improve.

And it would not take much for exogenous events – be they another unwelcome tariff surprise, or a shutdown in the Strait of Hormuz that sends crude oil prices into triple digits, or something else entirely that isn’t on anyone’s radar screen today – to push weak economic growth into negative growth. The odds of a recession are lower today than they were on April 3, but they are not zero.

Two, One or None

The FOMC’s median estimate for the Fed funds rate for 2025 reflected two rate cuts between now and the end of the year, the same as the median estimate in March. So, no change, right? Not so fast. The median is simply the number smack in the middle of nineteen individual guesses.

In March, four of the nineteen FOMC members projected that there would be no further rate cuts this year. In the June SEP, though, the number of “no more cuts” estimates rose to seven. Which makes sense, given the Fed’s commitment to bringing inflation back to the long-term two percent target, and given that it currently doesn’t see that target being finally reached until 2028.

The Fed meets again in July, but there won’t be another set of Summary Economic Projections until September. We expect the Committee members will have quite a bit of new data to digest between now and then, particularly with regard to inflation.

As Fed chair Powell said during the post-meeting press conference on Wednesday (and as we noted in our commentary last week), the full effect of inflation from increased tariffs, which up to now has been relatively quiescent, is likely to come into view during the next several months.

By the time the FOMC meets in September, they may have a better sense of whether the higher inflation will be more transitory in nature or something to worry about for a longer time frame.

If it looks transitory, then the focus will likely turn back to slower growth and higher unemployment, which in turn would give a higher likelihood of at least one, if not two of those rate cuts coming into effect. Either way, this is likely to be a very data-rich summer, with plenty of implications for portfolio positioning.

data.title: Teleperformance: The 'Future Forward' Plan Attests Our Buy Rating
data.description:
Although we maintained a Strong Buy Recommendation, we had previously taken a cautious view, choosing to wait for clearer signals at the Capital Markets Day. The latest developments have made that stance appear less justified than we had expected, particularly as the shares just posted their worst day since March 2024 when the Klarna release triggered a sharp sell-off.

Following the Capital Markets Day, it is essential that we revisit our financial assumptions and present what we consider to be a fair and updated estimate of Teleperformance’s (OTCPK:TLPFF) (OTCPK:TLPFY) outlook. As a reminder, our rating was backed by 1) AI Upside To Price In, 2) Specialized Services segment development with higher EBITDA contribution, 3) a value-creative acquisition (Majorel), and 4) a double-digit FCF yield with downside protection due to a supportive shareholders' remuneration. So far, we have not been fortunate with our timing, and since our last Q1 update, Teleperformance’s share price has declined by 8.4% (Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Capital Market Day Insight with Our View

Teleperformance announced its Future Forward strategic plan (Fig. 2), outlining:

  1. Organic revenue growth target from +4% to +6% by 2028. The company reached a €10.28 billion turnover in 2024. This would imply a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 1.32% per year at the low end (4% total growth) and 1.96% per year at the high end (6% total growth). In numbers, this would estimate sales between €10.83 billion and €11.10 billion by 2028. Our first revision is to reduce the company’s 2025 revenue estimate, reflecting a more cautious stance on near-term growth following the Capital Markets Day. The company reported a 1.6% year-over-year increase in Q1 sales, but we have now revised our yearly sales forecast from €10.8 billion to €10.4 billion. This already includes the TLS Visa application sales impact;
  2. Recurring EBITA margin at approximately 15.5% post-AI transformation. Again, the company reported a 2024 EBITA margin of 15%. We view this outlook as weak. Seeing increased downside risks, primarily driven by disruption linked to the adoption of AI and the potential erosion of its traditional service model. The management outlook is shifting towards AI as a more defensive approach than an offensive one. This was not well-received by the market. Having said that, this situation feels remarkably similar to what we experienced 20 years ago with IT outsourcing. Technological disruption brings complexity, and many players struggle to cope with it. Having said that, implementing AI is far from plug-and-play, and to leverage AI capabilities fully, clients need robust infrastructure, starting with cloud adoption, which remains limited. Moreover, effective AI deployment depends on stringent data governance and compliance, as well as selecting and customizing the right language model from a vast and fragmented landscape. Given these technical and operational complexities, the rollout of AI solutions is likely to be more gradual than the market fears. This is our view. The implied CAGR for recurring EBITA margin from 2024 to 2028 — rising from 15.0% to 15.5% — is approximately +0.82% per year. This is a low number, which is already incorporated in our estimates. Therefore, we confirm our internal EBITA projection of 15.1% in 2025; however, we lower the absolute value due to lower sales. So, our EBITA reached €1.57 billion (from €1.63 billion);
  3. Free cash flow of approximately €3 billion over the period, with a capital allocation of 50% of FCF. Dividends and buybacks will distribute this. There is no change to our previous forecast, and we see further upside potential through buybacks. Given the lower share price, the buyback program offers increased upside. As a result, we expect the company to accelerate repurchases and further reduce its shares outstanding over the coming quarters;
  4. AI & transformation investments: 20% of free cash flow directed toward technology initiatives. We might perceive AI investment as a stopgap against lost business, which raises questions about where organic growth will come from. While disruption across the sector is inevitable, the company is actively investing to position itself for future growth and remain competitive in a rapidly evolving landscape As a reminder, Teleperformance signed "partnerships with two leading Agentic AI companies — Ema and Parloa — as part of its broader investment program, which is set to allocate up to €100 million in 2025". Additionally, the company boasts an average client relationship duration of 13 years (Fig. 3) — a rare level of customer retention in the sector, and there is balance sheet support for AI investment and M&A for consolidation.

Source: Teleperformance Capital Market Day ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Based on the considerations outlined above, we now revise our estimates to report sales and EBITA at €10.4 billion and €1.57 billion, respectively. FCF remains unchanged, and considering the latest refinancing, we estimate the annual interest rate to be €200 million. This implies a €500 deleverage process, and we believe the company will accelerate its buyback given the depressed share price environment. In France, the corporate income tax rate is 25%. Considering Teleperformance’s large company status, an additional 3.3% social contribution tax applies. However, the company's effective tax rate is typically 25.5%, so we project a net profit forecast of €940 million, down from a previous estimate of €1.03 billion. Given the current share numbers (59.87 million), Teleperformance's EPS decreased to €15.70 from €17.20.

Currently, the company trades at a P/E <5x with an EV/EBITA <3x. We have also achieved an FCF yield of over 20%. Last time, for consistency reasons, we applied a 13x P/E target. Even using a P/E ratio of 8x, our valuation would reach €125 per share, representing a 63% upside ($77 in ADR). This P/E target is materially lower than the past 5-year average of 11x. Therefore, we confirm our Buy.

Additionally, we do not see any price AI upside, nor does the company's ability to navigate different markets (IT outsourcing - Fig. 4) appear promising. Furthermore, we have not seen a reduction in its share number despite the ongoing buyback.

Fig 4

Risks and Threats

We have extensive coverage of the company, so we report the following risks: 1) AI disruption with the company's inability in product innovation, 2) higher than estimated lower sales from the Specialized Services division such as the non-renewal of the TLS contract, 3) incumbent players with new AI technology, 4) continued CAPEX and M&A investment with failure to adopt and integrate emerging technologies, 5) FX changes and wage inflation, 6) restructuring initiatives (in Spain and France), 7) negative Wall Street sentiment with lower earnings revision, and 8) regulatory changes. As a reminder, Teleperformance is subject to strict U.S. regulations regarding the "Do Not Call List."

Conclusion

Last time, we concluded the analysis with this sentence:

There appear to be two opposing views on GenAI’s impact on the customer experience industry: one suggesting it undermines the very foundation of the CX business model, and another viewing it as a new tool that companies in the sector will integrate, making the recent derating unjustified. We align with the latter perspective."

Today, even if we believe that AI will be incremental, the company is priced with an EV/EBITA lower than 3x, representing a distressed level valuation. This is not our base case, and we reiterate our buy conviction.
